coredata.dc:title: Cluster beam microfabrication of SiC pattern on Si(100)

coredata.dc:description: We report the observation by scanning electron microscopy, spatially resolved Auger electron spectroscopy, and atomic force microscopy of the thermal evolution of a microsized array of carbon dots, fabricated using supersonic cluster beam deposition through a stencil mask. We show that it is possible to obtain ordered arrays of silicon carbide microsized dots by reaching the temperature necessary for the creation of Si–C bonds, without affecting the geometry of the pristine pattern.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
Atomic force microscopy
Carbon
Silicon carbide
Clusters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of a portion of the pattern of ns-carbon dots measured (a) at RT and (b) after annealing at ≈1100 °C. SR-AES spectra taken from selected points of the sample (c) at RT and (d) after annealing at ≈1100 °C.
Auger integrated spectra (a) C KVV and (b) Si L 2,3 VV normalized at their maximum intensity after the subtraction of an integrated background.
(a) AFM image of a dot after annealing at 1100 °C, (b) profile taken across the dot and (c) image obtained inverting the image (a).

head.ce:title: Cluster beam microfabrication of SiC pattern on Si(100)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratorio Nazionale TASC-INFM, Strada Statale 14, Km.163.5, Basovizza, I-34012 Trieste, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: INFM and Dipartimento di Matematica e Fisica, Universita’, Cattolica del Sacro Cuore,Via dei Musei 41, I-25121 Brescia, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: INFM-Dipartimento di Fisica, Universita’ di Milano, Via Celoria 16, I-20133 Milano, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the observation by scanning electron microscopy, spatially resolved Auger electron spectroscopy, and atomic force microscopy of the thermal evolution of a microsized array of carbon dots, fabricated using supersonic cluster beam deposition through a stencil mask. We show that it is possible to obtain ordered arrays of silicon carbide microsized dots by reaching the temperature necessary for the creation of Si–C bonds, without affecting the geometry of the pristine pattern.

ce:sections.ce:para: The growth and processing of silicon carbide for the fabrication of high temperature microelectronics devices and microelectromechanical systems because of its remarkable physical properties such as wide band gap, high thermal conductivity, chemical inertness, hardness, and wear resistance have received a conspicuous interest [1].

Applications of SiC-based structures such as high-frequency signal processing components, sensors, actuators, require patterning capabilities at the micro- and nanoscale [2]. The high chemical stability of SiC makes the use of conventional wet etching techniques difficult [1]. Dry etching techniques such as plasma-based reactive ion etching (RIE) [3] and anisotropic electron cyclotron resonance (ECR) plasma etching [4] have been used to pattern SiC structures. These methods suffer from several drawbacks: RIE often results in surface damage as well as contamination from the cathode material through micromasking effects [3]. ECR plasma etching is applied on surfaces prepared by electron beam lithography and lift-off processes. This makes the ECR approach coupled to electron beam lithography expensive and not suited for batch fabrication. Recently RIE has been integrated with nonmetallic etch masks demonstrating the possibility of integrating etching processes into conventional microfabrication technologies [5].

Alternative approaches have been proposed based on direct growth of SiC patterned structures on silicon substrates where carbon containing precursors are deposited and react at high temperatures. Seeded supersonic molecular beams (SSMB) [6] can be used to carry on Si surfaces precursors such as methylsilane (SiH3CH3) molecules [3] and fullerenes [7,8]. SSMB has the advantage, compared to effusive beams, to accelerate the carried species to relatively high translational kinetic energies (from fractions of eV up to several tens of eV) thus requiring a lower substrate temperature for the growth of SiC [8].

In order to form patterned structures SSMB must be coupled to e-beam lithography: silicon surface is oxide or hydride passivated prior to deposition, an electron beam is then scanned on the passivated substrate in order to locally remove the passivating species and form patterned reactive area ready for the high temperature SiC formation.

Exploiting the extremely low divergence of supersonic cluster beams [9] it is possible to deposit patterns composed by cluster-assembled structures through a stencil mask [10]. The final lateral resolution of the pattern can be as low as few hundreds of nanometers for objects with a very high aspect ratio [9,10].

Recently we have shown that thermal annealing of nanostructured carbon films pre-deposited onto the Si(100) surface promotes the formation of a SiC film [11] in analogy to what observed with fullerenes.

In this letter we show it is possible to fabricate ordered arrays of micron-sized SiC dots on Si(100) surfaces by annealing cluster-assembled carbon dots previously deposited through a stencil mask. The modification of nanostructured carbon and the formation of SiC upon annealing have been investigated by spatially resolved electronic spectroscopy and atomic force microscopy (AFM) with particular attention to the influence of high temperatures on the pattern integrity and lateral resolution of the pristine carbon dots.

Carbon dots arrays have been fabricated by supersonic cluster beam deposition on a Si(100) substrate sonicated in acetone before deposition preserving the native oxide on the top of the surface. The method is described in detail in Ref. [10]. As a stencil mask we have used a commercial electron grid for transmission electron microscopy.

After deposition the sample was transferred for characterization into an ultra-high vacuum apparatus consisting of a preparation chamber (base pressure<2.0×10−10 mbar) and a measurement chamber (base pressure<3.0×10−11 mbar) equipped with a scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a spatially resolved Auger electron spectroscopy (SR-AES) facility. The sample was directly annealed at selected temperatures ranging from room temperature (RT) to 1100 °C. The sample was held at each temperature for about 1 h. The temperature was measured with an infrared pyrometer (RAYTEK, Marathon-MA2-C).

SEM and SR-AES characterizations have been performed using a FEI electron-gun with a nominal minimum beam diameter of 20 nm and an estimated effective lateral resolution of the order of some tens of nanometer. The typical working conditions used for the measurements presented here are: beam voltage 15 kV and beam current 2 nA. The SEM images are acquired collecting both the secondaries and backscattered electrons.

The electron analyzer is a cylindrical sector analyzer operated in constant pass energy mode. Si L 2,3 VV and C KVV integrated spectra were measured both on the top of the dots and in the regions between the dots.

AFM measurements were also performed on the samples before and after the process of thermal heating, using a commercial AFM (Digital Instruments Multimode IIIa), operated in air in both tapping and contact mode.

Fig. 1a shows a SEM micrograph of a portion of the as-deposited patterned region. The typical diameter of a dot, measured by AFM is 40 μm and the average height is about 120 nm. The roughness measured on the dots is very high, about 80 nm. This large roughness is typical of a carbon cluster beam having a lognormal distribution of masses with average mass at about 900 carbon atoms per cluster [9].

Roughness of ns-carbon films is characterized by a hierarchical organization of features of different size, from the single cluster and cluster aggregates to larger and larger agglomerates, spanning up to three decades in size [12]. For small thickness of the film, the relative weight of roughness (the ratio of twice the roughness over the film thickness) can be even larger than unity.

In Fig. 1(a) and (b) we report the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of a portion of the pattern of ns-carbon dots measured at RT and after annealing at 1100 °C, respectively. A set of SR-AES spectra taken from selected points of the samples shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b) are presented in Fig. 1(c) and (d).

In Fig. 1(a) the dots appear dark while the regions among the dots are white. By analyzing the chemical composition of the dots and of the regions between the dots by means of SR-AES, we have observed that at RT the only detected signal on the dot is due to the presence of C, while in the regions between the dots, both C and Si signals are detected as shown in Fig. 1(c). This is consistent with the AFM observations.

The observed contrast in the SEM image (see Fig. 1(a)) can be explained considering the difference in the atomic number Z (average atomic number in the case of the region between the dots). Because of the nearly monotonic increase of the backscatter coefficient with Z, the regions of high average atomic number appear bright relative to regions of low atomic number. At this beam energy (15 kV) the contribution from the secondary electrons is, to a first approximation, similar for the two materials, because the secondary electron coefficient is not a strong function of the atomic number Z.

When the sample is annealed at 1100 °C the contrast of the SEM images (Fig. 1(b)) is suddenly inverted: the dots now appear white while the regions in between are darker. The SR-AES measurements show at this step of annealing the presence of C and Si signals both coming from the dots and from the regions between the dots.

We observe marked differences in the C KVV Auger line shape between the carbon at RT and after annealing at 1100 °C, both on the dots and in between the dots. This is shown in detail in Fig. 2 where we report the integrated spectra of the C KVV and Si L 2,3 VV Auger emission on the dot (see bottom of Fig. 2(a) and (b)) and on the region between the dots (see top of Fig. 2(a) and (b)) both at RT and after annealing at 1100 °C. In order to better put in evidence the line shape changes, the spectra have been normalized at their maximum intensity after the subtraction of an integrated background.

The spectral line shape of the Auger C KVV emission is known to reflect the chemical environment (bonding) local to the C sites [13]. It is clear that the carbon bonding at RT is graphitic as already observed in recent photoemission measurements performed on carbon nanoclusters films [11]. After annealing at 1100 °C both on the flat regions and on the dots the C KVV line shape changes in agreement with the formation of a carbidic bonding with Si [14].

After annealing at 1100 °C the Si L 2,3 VV signal from the dot changes with respect to the spectrum measured from the flat region as can be seen in Fig. 2(b): the main peak is broader and the shoulder at ≈70 eV kinetic energy is shifted at lower kinetic energy. Since the Si L 2,3 VV Auger line shape reflects a band-like character, the line shape modifications of Si L 2,3 VV spectrum mirror the valence band DOS changes local to the Si sites. In this connection the Si L 2,3 VV signal can be used as a fingerprint to identify silicon bonds with other species [15].
The Si L 2,3 VV from the dot has the line shape typical of Si–C bonding [16,17], while the signal from the flat regions is primarily due to Si–Si bonding [17,18].

Therefore, after annealing at 1100 °C, the chemical scenario appears to be the following: the dots are characterized by the formation of carbidic bonds, while the regions between them expose prevalently the clean silicon surface, with the presence of isolated carbon clusters bound to the Si substrate atoms through the formation of carbidic bonds.

The inverted contrast in the SEM images after annealing at 1100 °C (Fig. 1b) is not consistent with a compositional contrast. This is a typical case where secondary electron signals can vary strongly with very small changes in composition [19]. It was observed indeed, that the expected atomic number contrast between the silicon (white) and the silicon carbide (dark) is observed when only backscattered electrons are detected, while the corresponding secondary plus backscattered electron image shows reversed contrast between the silicon (black) and silicon carbide (white) [19].

AFM characterization of the morphology of the dots after the thermal annealing shows that the lateral size of the dots is unchanged and their average height is 70–80 nm. Fig. 3(a) shows the image of a dot after annealing at 1100 °C. A typical profile taken across the dot is shown in Fig. 3(b).

The decrease in the height of dots after annealing can be ascribed both to the thermal-induced desorption of carbon atoms not strongly interacting with other carbon atoms and/or with the substrate and to the rearrangement of carbon atoms after the interaction with the Si atoms interdiffused in the bulk of the dots. The rms roughness is about 80 nm on the dots, and 60 nm in the region between.

Small pits with a lateral dimension of few hundreds of nanometers and up to 200 nm deep can be seen all around the dots forming a ring close to the SiC–Si boundary (Fig. 3c). This image was obtained inverting the original image Fig. 3(a), such that pits appeared as protrusions, while protrusions (and the dot itself) shifted on the negative axis. Thresholding the image allowed to retain only the points on the positive axis, providing the pits.

The presence of pits has a fundamental role in the carburization of the material forming the dots [20,21]. At the very first stage, the silicon atoms at the dot/silicon substrate interface react with the carbon clusters forming a thin SiC layer. Annealing the sample promotes a temperature-driven enhancement of the silicon diffusion from the substrate, through the newly formed SiC film, toward the nanostructured carbon overlayers. This silicon diffusion sustains the carburization reaction.

A thicker SiC layer is thus formed. Being the diffusion coefficient for Si through bulk SiC very low [22], other growth mechanisms rather than Si diffusion through the SiC layer may dominate. One mechanism is the formation of pits at the silicon–SiC interface, which continue through the film up to the surface (we observe them at the boundary of the dots, see Fig. 3), representing diffusion channels providing Si atoms for the reaction with the carbon overlayers [22].

We have observed that the carburization reaction of carbon clusters on Si(100) surface takes place at temperatures much lower than 1100 °C [23]. Annealing at 1100 °C is necessary in order to enhance the diffusion of Si into the thick dots and to carburize the entire original dot. It is very important to note that even at this high temperature the lateral resolution of the dots defined by the stencil mask is maintained in the SiC structure and no degradation of the pattern is observed. In the case of direct epitaxial SiC growth from gaseous precursors, films grown above 800 °C did not show any pattern [3]. This prevents the possibility of growing relatively thick structures.

In conclusion, we have shown that cluster-assembled carbon dot arrays deposited with a stencil mask on Si(100) can be carburized to produce ordered arrays of SiC dots. The high temperature (T≈1100 °C) necessary for the creation of Si–C bonds, does not affect the geometry of the pristine pattern thus allowing the fabrication of thick structures.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The nanospectroscopy facility in Brescia was funded by INFM under the Strumentazione Avanzata program. This research was partially funded under the FIRB project “Carbon-based nanostructures and microstructures”. The authors would like to thank M. Salerno for support in AFM imaging.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cluster Beam Synthesis of Nanostructured Materials
Crit. Rev. Solid State Mater. Sci.
Scanning Electron Microscopy and X-ray Microanalysis
